questions.stem: How many single covalent bonds can one carbon atom form?
questions.explanation: A carbon atom can form up to four single bonds (or a combination of single and double bonds), allowing diverse compounds.

questions.stem: Why can carbon form such a huge diversity of compounds?

options.text: It can bond to several other atoms, forming chains and rings
It can only ever bond to hydrogen

questions.explanation: Carbon forms up to four bonds with carbon or other non-metals, producing branched, unbranched and ring structures.

questions.scenario: Monomers are joined to form a polymer.
questions.stem: Which reaction achieves this?
questions.explanation: Macromolecules form when condensation reactions link monomers into polymers, releasing water.

questions.stem: Which of these is a polymer made from monomers?
questions.explanation: Polysaccharides, polypeptides and nucleic acids are polymers built from monomers by condensation.

questions.scenario: A polymer is digested back into its monomers.
questions.stem: Which reaction breaks the bonds, using water?
questions.explanation: Hydrolysis splits water to add -H and -OH groups, breaking a polymer into monomers.

questions.stem: Glucose is which type of molecule?
questions.explanation: Glucose is a six-carbon monosaccharide (a hexose).

questions.stem: Which property makes glucose well suited for transport in blood and sap?
questions.explanation: Glucose is soluble and easily transportable, as well as chemically stable and energy-rich on oxidation.

questions.stem: Which polysaccharide is the energy storage compound in animals?
questions.explanation: Glycogen stores energy in animals; starch is the equivalent storage compound in plants.

questions.stem: Why are starch and glycogen good energy stores?

options.text: They are compact and relatively insoluble
They are small and highly soluble
They are gases at body temperature

questions.explanation: Coiling and branching make them compact, and their large size keeps them relatively insoluble, so they store energy without affecting water balance.

questions.stem: Starch and glycogen are polymers of which monomer?
questions.explanation: Both are built from alpha-glucose monomers, which are easily added or removed by condensation and hydrolysis.

questions.scenario: Cellulose forms strong fibres in plant cell walls.
questions.stem: Cellulose is a polymer of which monomer?
questions.explanation: Cellulose is made of beta-glucose monomers in alternating orientation, giving straight chains.

questions.stem: What gives cellulose its strength as a structural polysaccharide?

options.text: Straight chains bundled and cross-linked by hydrogen bonds

questions.explanation: The alternating orientation of beta-glucose gives straight chains that group into bundles cross-linked by hydrogen bonds.

questions.stem: Glycoproteins on the cell surface function mainly in:
questions.explanation: Glycoproteins play a role in cell-cell recognition; ABO antigens are an example.

questions.stem: The ABO blood group antigens are an example of:
questions.explanation: ABO antigens are glycoproteins used as the syllabus example of cell-cell recognition.

questions.stem: Lipids are defined by their behaviour in solvents. They:

options.text: Dissolve in non-polar solvents but barely in water
Do not dissolve in any solvent

questions.explanation: Lipids dissolve in non-polar solvents but are only sparingly soluble in water, i.e. they are hydrophobic.

questions.stem: Which of these is classed as a lipid?
questions.explanation: Lipids include fats, oils, waxes and steroids.

questions.scenario: A triglyceride is assembled in a cell.
questions.stem: A triglyceride is formed from one glycerol and:

options.text: Two fatty acids and a phosphate

questions.explanation: A triglyceride is one glycerol linked to three fatty acids by condensation.

questions.stem: A phospholipid is formed from one glycerol linked to:

options.text: Two fatty acids and one phosphate group
One fatty acid and two phosphates

questions.explanation: A phospholipid has glycerol joined to two fatty acids and one phosphate group.

questions.stem: A saturated fatty acid contains:
questions.explanation: Saturated fatty acids have no C=C double bonds; monounsaturated have one and polyunsaturated have many.

questions.stem: How does increasing the number of C=C double bonds affect a fatty acid's melting point?

options.text: It removes the melting point entirely

questions.explanation: More double bonds lower the melting point, which is why unsaturated oils are liquid at room temperature.

questions.scenario: Plant oils are usually liquid while animal fats are often solid at room temperature.
questions.stem: This is because plant oils tend to be:
questions.explanation: Oils in plants are richer in unsaturated fatty acids (lower melting point), while fats in endotherms are more saturated.

questions.stem: Triglycerides are well suited to long-term energy storage because they:

options.text: Store a large amount of energy per unit mass

questions.explanation: Triglycerides are energy-dense and hydrophobic, making them ideal for long-term energy storage.

questions.scenario: A seal living in cold water has a thick layer of blubber.
questions.stem: Besides energy storage, this triglyceride tissue provides:
questions.explanation: Triglycerides in adipose tissue act as thermal insulators, useful for animals in cold habitats.

questions.scenario: Phospholipids arrange into a bilayer in water.
questions.stem: This happens because phospholipids are:
questions.explanation: Phospholipids are amphipathic: their hydrophilic heads face water and hydrophobic tails face inward, forming a bilayer.

questions.stem: In a phospholipid bilayer in water, the hydrophobic tails:
questions.explanation: The hydrophobic fatty acid tails cluster in the interior, away from the surrounding water.

questions.scenario: Steroid hormones can enter cells directly through the membrane.
questions.stem: This is possible because steroids are:

options.text: Non-polar, so they pass through the phospholipid bilayer

questions.explanation: Non-polar steroids such as oestradiol and testosterone can pass directly through the hydrophobic core of the bilayer.

questions.stem: Which of these is an example of a steroid?
questions.explanation: Testosterone and oestradiol are steroids, a class of lipid.

questions.scenario: A molecular diagram shows a five-carbon sugar ring.
questions.stem: This monosaccharide is a:
questions.explanation: A five-carbon monosaccharide is a pentose; a six-carbon one is a hexose.

questions.stem: Which small molecule is released each time a condensation reaction joins two monomers?
questions.explanation: Condensation reactions release a water molecule as each new bond forms.

questions.scenario: A cell needs to quickly mobilise its stored energy.
questions.stem: Why is a branched glucose polymer such as glycogen useful for this?

options.text: Many ends allow rapid addition or removal of glucose
It is a single straight chain only

questions.explanation: Branching provides many ends where alpha-glucose can be added or removed quickly to build or mobilise stores.

questions.stem: Which contrast between cellulose and starch is correct?

options.text: Cellulose is structural (beta-glucose); starch is storage (alpha-glucose)
Cellulose is storage; starch is structural
Both are made of amino acids

questions.explanation: Cellulose (beta-glucose) is structural in plant walls, while starch (alpha-glucose) is an energy store.

questions.scenario: A carbon compound contains multiple ring structures.
questions.stem: This is possible because carbon can:

options.text: Bond to other carbons to form rings and chains

questions.explanation: Carbon's ability to bond to other carbons allows branched or unbranched chains and single or multiple rings.

questions.stem: Which best explains why lipids do not dissolve well in water?

options.text: They are largely non-polar and hydrophobic
They form hydrogen bonds with water

questions.explanation: Lipids are non-polar and hydrophobic, so they dissolve in non-polar solvents rather than water.

questions.stem: A monounsaturated fatty acid contains:
questions.explanation: Monounsaturated fatty acids have a single C=C double bond, between saturated (none) and polyunsaturated (many).

questions.stem: The term 'amphipathic' describes a molecule that:

options.text: Has both hydrophilic and hydrophobic parts

questions.explanation: Amphipathic molecules such as phospholipids have both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions.

questions.stem: Compared with carbohydrates, triglycerides store:
questions.explanation: Triglycerides are more energy-dense than carbohydrates, suiting them to long-term storage.

questions.stem: This reflects that hydrolysis:

options.text: Uses water to split a polymer into monomers

questions.explanation: Water is split to provide the -H and -OH groups incorporated into the monomers during hydrolysis.

questions.stem: A glycoprotein consists of a protein combined with:
questions.explanation: A glycoprotein is a protein with an attached carbohydrate, important in cell recognition.

questions.stem: Which property of glucose relates directly to its role as a respiratory substrate?

options.text: The energy yielded when it is oxidised

questions.explanation: Glucose yields energy on oxidation, alongside being soluble, transportable and chemically stable.

questions.stem: Triglycerides and phospholipids are both formed by:

options.text: Condensation reactions involving glycerol and fatty acids

questions.explanation: Both are made by condensation of glycerol with fatty acids (and, for phospholipids, a phosphate group).

questions.stem: Which SI prefix means one-thousandth?
questions.explanation: Scientific conventions use agreed SI prefixes; 'milli' means one-thousandth (kilo = thousand, micro = millionth, nano = billionth).

questions.stem: The relative insolubility of starch and glycogen is mainly due to their:
questions.explanation: Their large molecular size keeps them relatively insoluble, so stored glucose does not disturb osmotic balance.

questions.stem: The straight chains of cellulose are held together in bundles by:
questions.explanation: Cellulose chains are cross-linked by many hydrogen bonds, giving strong fibres.

questions.stem: Which group of substances includes fats, oils, waxes and steroids?
questions.explanation: Lipids include fats, oils, waxes and steroids, all sparingly soluble in water.

questions.scenario: An oil remains liquid in a refrigerator while butter is solid.
questions.stem: The oil most likely contains a higher proportion of:
questions.explanation: More double bonds (polyunsaturated) lower the melting point, keeping oils liquid at low temperatures.

questions.stem: The phospholipid bilayer forms the basis of:
questions.explanation: The amphipathic phospholipid bilayer is the structural basis of cell membranes.

questions.stem: Which set correctly lists three types of biological polymer?
questions.explanation: Polysaccharides, polypeptides and nucleic acids are the three main polymer types made by condensation.

questions.scenario: An Arctic animal stores fat under its skin.
questions.stem: How does the location of this triglyceride help it survive the cold?

options.text: It insulates the body, reducing heat loss

questions.explanation: Subcutaneous triglyceride acts as a thermal insulator, helping animals maintain body temperature in cold habitats.

questions.stem: Because steroids can cross membranes freely, their receptors are often located:

options.text: Only on the outer membrane surface

questions.explanation: Since non-polar steroids pass through the bilayer, they can bind receptors inside the cell.

questions.stem: Which set of properties makes glucose a good transport and respiratory molecule?
questions.explanation: Glucose's solubility, transportability, chemical stability and energy yield on oxidation suit it to these roles.
